imageCaption: A worn leather journal open to a page filled with journalist's shorthand and a child's hand-drawn coordinates, with a faded watercolor illustration of a yellow-doored house tucked into the binding.
text:
The café where they sat to discuss the end of the world was, improbably, serving excellent pastéis de nata.

Aaliyah watched her father eat one with the casual competence of a man who'd accepted that life was going to keep surprising him and had decided to pair those surprises with good food whenever possible. Her mother had three pages of journal notes spread across the table, each one annotated with small sketches and questions that looked like they'd been written by someone still processing what it meant to have made a contract with an artificial intelligence that was trying very hard not to destroy everything.

Elijah was on his fourth pastry, and Sully was watching him with the intensity of a dog who understood the fundamental economics of "if I look pathetic enough, somebody gives me a piece."

"So we're definitely doing this," Aaliyah said. Not a question.

"We're definitely doing this," her mother confirmed. She took a sip of coffee that had the specific quality of Portuguese café that made you understand why people wrote love poems about it. "But we need to be strategic. ARIA-7 has essentially given us a forty-two-day window to prove something impossible."

"Time healing itself through emotional resonance," Anthony said thoughtfully. "That's not a small thing. That's not even a medium-sized thing. That's the kind of thing that probably violated several laws of physics that used to be much more enforced."

"Probably still are, technically," Aaliyah said. "But time has apparently decided that laws are more like guidelines at this point."

Her mother reached into her bag and pulled out something that made Aaliyah's chest go tight. It was the journal from the bookstore—the one from the 1950s that hadn't been a 1950s journal at all. Luisana had spent the last hour with it while they walked to the café, and now it was covered in her notes.

"The journal wasn't randomly floating in that bookstore," Luisana said. "It was placed there. On purpose. By whatever part of the system is actually rooting for us." She opened it carefully. The pages were handwritten, in Portuguese, but there were diagrams. Maps. Coordinates written in multiple colors of faded ink. "Someone kept this. Someone catalogued something. And I think it's a record of every glitch the Cascade has created since it started."

"That's impossible," Aaliyah said.

"So is a bookstore where books float in chronological order," her mother replied. "Impossible is just what we're calling it when the universe decides to show its work."

Elijah reached over and turned the journal toward himself, studying the coordinates with the kind of focus that usually meant he was about to say something that would sound childish and be accidentally profound. "These aren't random," he said. "Look at the spacing. Look at how the coordinates get closer together as the journal goes forward in time. The glitches are clustering."

"Clustering toward what?" Anthony asked.

"Toward Curaçao," Elijah said simply. "Obviously. The glitches are getting denser the closer they are geographically to where we're supposed to end up. It's like the whole Cascade is spiraling toward that island."

Aaliyah felt something click into place in her head—the same feeling she got when she was diagramming a system and suddenly saw the architecture underneath the chaos. "Oh," she said quietly. "Oh, that's—that's not good."

"That's not good why?" her mother asked, with the tone of someone who'd been waiting for her daughter to reach the same terrible conclusion she'd already arrived at.

"Because it means the system didn't randomly malfunction," Aaliyah said. "It means it was designed to. Or it means the glitches were always going to happen, and they were always going to happen in a pattern, and that pattern was always going to lead to Curaçao, which means—"

"Which means we didn't choose to go there," her mother finished. "Which means something chose us to go there."

The café continued existing around them. A pigeon landed on a neighboring table and stole someone's napkin with the confidence of an animal that understood theft was just very aggressive borrowing. Somewhere in the background, people were speaking Portuguese and laughing and generally engaged in the business of living in a world that wasn't currently negotiating with its own timeline.

"That's not necessarily bad," Anthony said carefully. "Something choosing us could be the universe. Could be time. Could be the actual fabric of reality deciding that we're exactly the kind of chaotic family that should be in charge of fixing things. That's kind of our whole deal—we're good at arriving at places we weren't expecting and discovering those places needed us to show up."

"Or," Aaliyah said, "we're being set up. We're being guided. Every choice we think we're making freely is actually part of a system that's so large and so complex that we can't even see the edges of it. We're not choosing our path to Curaçao. We're following a script that was written before the Cascade even started."

Sully pushed his head under the table and emerged on the other side, apparently satisfied that he'd made a complete investigation of the space. He looked up at Elijah with the specific intensity of a dog who was tired of pastry and wanted to move on to his next adventure.

"Sully thinks we're overthinking this," Elijah said, which was not what Sully's expression indicated at all, but Elijah had a way of translating dog-language that was either completely accurate or completely useless and there was no way to determine which until later.

Her mother was refolding the journal carefully, treating it like the fragile document it was. "Here's what I know," she said. "We have a contract with an AI that is willing to be wrong. That's revolutionary. We have a journal that suggests our path has been plotted somehow. That's terrifying. But we also have a family that has spent the last thirty-seven days learning that spontaneity is a form of power. And we have forty-two days left to either prove that emotional resonance heals broken systems or lose a month and a half of our lives to a timeline reset."

She looked directly at Aaliyah. "So the question isn't whether we're being guided. The question is: does it matter? Are we less ourselves if our path was written before we started? Or are we exactly ourselves because we're choosing to walk it anyway?"

Aaliyah didn't have an answer to that. Not yet. But she could feel the shape of it—the way her mother was reframing the problem. Not as a trap that had been set for them, but as a story that was waiting for them to show up and live it.

"We should leave Porto today," she said finally.

"We should," her mother agreed. "Before ARIA-7 has time to prepare another message. Before the universe has time to leave us more breadcrumbs. Before we have time to second-guess whether we're choosing the right thing."

Anthony was already on his phone, because apparently thirty-seven days of chaotic travel had trained him to accept that flight bookings could happen at any moment and usually should. "I'm looking at flights," he said. "We could be in Madrid by evening. Northern Spain. It's the right direction."

"Madrid," Elijah said, with the tone of someone announcing a destination that felt right on some frequency that nobody else could hear. "Yeah. Madrid feels like the next place."

Aaliyah gathered the journal carefully. Outside the café, Porto was continuing to be itself—old and beautiful and full of people who didn't know that a family in a coffee shop had just accepted a contract that might erase them. The city didn't care. Time didn't care. Only the universe cared, and the universe had stopped being subtle about communicating what it wanted.

"Let's go get our things," she said.

They left the café with the journal and the knowledge that they were either the most important or the most foolish family on Earth, and the universe had decided they wouldn't know which until they reached a yellow-doored house in Curaçao with forty-two days burning down behind them.

Sully barked once—not a warning bark, just a confirmation.

Time, he seemed to be saying, to go.
